title: The Polyvagal Theory and Kundalini: Stephen Porges' Work Applied to Spiritual Emergency

body:
The Polyvagal Theory and Kundalini: Stephen Porges' Work Applied to Spiritual Emergency

You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, staring at the ceiling, feeling like your insides are a live wire, and your brain's running a marathon it can't finish. You're searching, desperate for something, anything, to make sense of the chaos in your head and body. I get it. I've been there. That deep, gnawing terror that makes you question everything you thought was real. This isn't some abstract concept for me; it's my life, and probably yours right now. You're likely experiencing what some call a spiritual emergency, and if you've stumbled upon "kundalini," you're probably feeling even more alone and misunderstood. But here's the thing: it's not just "spiritual." It's deeply, irrevocably physiological. And that's where Stephen Porges' Polyvagal Theory comes in. It's not a magic bullet, but it's a map. And when you're lost in the dark, a map is everything.

You're not crazy. That's the first thing you need to hear. Your body isn't betraying you, not really. It's reacting. It's trying to protect you, even if it feels like it's tearing you apart. What you're going through - the intense anxiety, the panic attacks that come out of nowhere, the bizarre physical sensations, the feeling of being utterly disconnected or overwhelmingly connected to everything all at once - it's all deeply tied to your nervous system. And for a long time, the spiritual community, hell, even the psychological community, didn't have a good way to talk about this. They'd say "it's your ego dying" or "you're purging old energy." And while there's a grain of truth in that, it doesn't give you anything concrete to hold onto when you feel like you're drowning. Polyvagal Theory gives you that.

Understanding Your Nervous System in Crisis

Look, you've probably heard of "fight or flight." Everyone has. It's that adrenaline surge, the heart pounding, the urge to run or punch something. That's one part of your nervous system, the sympathetic branch. But it's not the whole story, not by a long shot. Porges introduced us to a more complex view of the vagus nerve, which is a big, wandering nerve that connects your brain to almost every organ in your body. It's like the main highway for your internal state. And it has two main branches that react very differently to threat.

The Ventral Vagal State: Connection and Safety

This is your happy place, your calm, regulated state. When you're in ventral vagal, you feel safe, connected, able to engage with others, laugh, learn, feel empathy. Your heart rate is steady, your breathing is easy, your digestion works. You can make eye contact, your voice has inflection. This is where you want to be. This is where you feel like you. When you're having a good day, when you're with people you trust, when you feel at ease - that's your ventral vagal system humming along. It's designed for social engagement, for feeling safe in the world. But when kundalini kicks in, or when you're in a spiritual emergency, this state can feel impossibly far away. You might get glimpses, tiny windows of peace, but they're often quickly swallowed by the other states.

The Dorsal Vagal State: Shutdown and Dissociation

This is the one that gets really tricky, especially with spiritual emergencies. When fight or flight isn't an option - think of a prey animal caught by a predator - the body's oldest defense system kicks in: the dorsal vagal response. This isn't fight or flight; it's freeze. It's collapse. It's dissociation. Your heart rate can drop, your breathing becomes shallow, you might feel numb, faint, or completely disconnected from your body and the world. You might feel a sense of dread, hopelessness, or deep emptiness. This is your body's attempt to conserve energy and minimize pain when escape isn't possible. It's like playing dead. And in a spiritual emergency, this can manifest as feeling completely out of your body, unable to move, unable to speak, feeling like you're dying, or just… gone. You know that feeling when you're so overwhelmed you just want to curl up and disappear? That's dorsal vagal. And when it's extreme, it's terrifying. I've been there, feeling like I was just a ghost in my own skin, watching the world go by, utterly unable to interact. It's a kind of living death, honestly. And it's often mistaken for some deep spiritual "void" experience when it's actually your nervous system in extreme shutdown.

Kundalini and the Nervous System Rollercoaster

So, you've got this kundalini thing, right? Or you suspect you do. You've read The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) and you're seeing yourself in those pages. The energy surges, the heat, the cold, the vibrations, the visions, the intense emotional swings. It's not just your mind; it's your entire being. And it's hitting your nervous system like a freight train. What I've found, and what many others in this space have experienced, is that kundalini often throws you into a rapid, unpredictable oscillation between these different nervous system states.

  • Sympathetic Overdrive: You're wired, anxious, can't sleep, heart racing, mind buzzing with thoughts, feeling impending doom. That's fight or flight in full swing. Your body perceives the intense internal energy as a threat, and it reacts accordingly.
  • Dorsal Vagal Shutdown: Then, suddenly, you might crash. You feel utterly drained, numb, depressed, disconnected. You can't move, can't think, just want to disappear. This is your system going into freeze mode, trying to conserve what little energy it has left from the sympathetic onslaught.
  • Brief Glimpses of Ventral Vagal: Sometimes, you get a moment of peace. A sudden feeling of deep love, connection, or clarity. These are precious. These are your ventral vagal system briefly coming online, showing you what's possible. But they're often fleeting, quickly replaced by the terror or the numbness.

This constant, jarring shift is incredibly disorienting and exhausting. It's why you feel so utterly out of control. Your body is trying to process an experience it wasn't designed for, or at least, wasn't designed for in such an intense, uncontrolled way. It's why many people feel like they're going insane. You're not. Your nervous system is just overwhelmed. You know what I mean? It's like your internal thermostat is broken, constantly flipping between extreme heat and extreme cold.

Regulating Your System: Practical Steps

So, what do you do when you're caught in this physiological maelstrom? The goal isn't to stop kundalini - you can't, not really. The goal is to regulate your nervous system, to create more moments of ventral vagal safety, and to gently guide yourself out of prolonged states of sympathetic panic or dorsal shutdown. It's about building resilience in your system so it can better handle the intense energies. This isn't a quick fix, but it's a path forward. You've got to be patient with yourself, incredibly so.

  • Safety First: The absolute first step is to recognize where you are. Are you in panic (sympathetic)? Are you shut down (dorsal)? Are you reasonably calm (ventral)? Just knowing helps. Then, create as much external safety as you possibly can. This means minimizing stressors, finding people you trust (even if it's just one person you can call at 3 AM), and making your physical environment as soothing as possible. Turn off the news, put on soft lighting, wrap yourself in a blanket. Your nervous system needs to feel safe.
  • Grounding Techniques: When you're in sympathetic overdrive or dorsal shutdown, you're usually disconnected from your body. Grounding brings you back. Focus on your senses:
    • Sight: Look around your room. Name five things you can see. Notice their colors, shapes, textures.
    • Sound: Listen to the sounds around you. The hum of the fridge, traffic outside, your own breathing. Just notice, don't judge.
    • Touch: Feel your feet on the floor, your clothes on your skin, the texture of a blanket. Hold an ice cube in your hand, or splash cold water on your face. The shock can sometimes jolt you back into your body.

    These aren't deep spiritual practices; they're nervous system hacks. They tell your brain, "Hey, I'm here, I'm real, and I'm safe enough right now."

  • Breathwork - Gentle, Not Forceful: Forget the intense pranayama for now. When your system is dysregulated, strong breathwork can actually trigger more panic. Focus on slow, gentle, diaphragmatic breathing. Inhale for a count of 4, hold for 2, exhale for 6. Make your exhale longer than your inhale. This activates the vagus nerve and signals safety to your brain. You can even try humming or chanting - the vibrations stimulate the vagus nerve directly.
  • Movement - Gentle, Not Strenuous: Again, no crazy workouts. Think gentle walks in nature, slow stretching, tai chi, or even just swaying your body side to side. Movement helps discharge pent-up energy from the sympathetic system and can bring you back into your body from dorsal shutdown.
  • Co-regulation: This is huge. If you have someone safe - a friend, a therapist, a partner - just being in their calm presence can help regulate your system. Their regulated nervous system can subtly help yours find its way back. This is why having support is so, so important during a spiritual emergency. You can't do this alone. If you haven't already, you need to find people who understand, people who won't just dismiss you as crazy. You might find some guidance in When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link).
  • Vagus Nerve Stimulation: There are devices out there, like a Vagus Nerve Stimulator - Portable TENS Unit (paid link), that can gently stimulate the vagus nerve. I've used them. They're not a cure, but they can be a tool to help calm your system down when you're really struggling. Always do your research and consult with a doctor before using any new device, though.

The Path to Integration, Not Suppression

You're not trying to shut down the spiritual experience; you're trying to integrate it. You're trying to build a nervous system that's strong enough, flexible enough, to handle the intensity without constantly spiraling into terror or collapse. It's like training a muscle. You start small, you go slow, and you celebrate every tiny step forward. Every time you bring yourself back from the brink, even for a moment, you're strengthening your capacity.

Here's the truth: this is hard. It's messy. It's often terrifying. I'll tell you what, there were nights I thought I wouldn't make it. But you can. You absolutely can. Understanding your nervous system isn't some academic exercise when you're in the thick of it; it's a lifeline. It gives you agency. It gives you something to do when you feel utterly helpless. Does that land? Because I know that feeling of helplessness, of being a leaf in a hurricane.

You're not broken. You're just experiencing something incredibly intense, and your body is reacting exactly as it's designed to react to perceived threat. The spiritual journey, especially one involving kundalini, can trick your system into thinking it's under attack, even when the "threat" is internal energy. Your job now is to gently reassure your system, signal safety, and slowly, patiently, build your capacity to hold this energy. You can get through this. You really can. And you don't have to pretend it's all rainbows and light when it feels like hell. Acknowledge the fear, acknowledge the pain, and then take one tiny, regulating step. One breath. One grounding exercise. One moment of gentle self-compassion. That's how you begin to find your way back.

If you're feeling completely overwhelmed and alone, know that there are resources out there beyond just me. Look up organizations that deal with spiritual emergence and emergency. Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) is a classic for a reason. You're not the first, and you won't be the last. But you have tools now. You have a map. Use it. Be kind to yourself. This isn't a race; it's a marathon. And you're stronger than you think.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
